figures.caption: Figure 1: Graded allocation improves full-context and passage-budget frontiers. It does not materially improve retrieval-call AUC because k = 1 is still a retrieval call.
Table 2: Binary vs. graded frontier AUC on TriviaQA. Deltas are relative to each signal’s binary call-AUC.

Table 6: Retriever and corpus robustness. Wiki uses a 3.5M-passage index. Harm is closed-bookcorrect/open-book-wrong; Saved is the fraction of harmful retrievals avoided at the selected operating point.
Figure 6: Matched-reader decision-signal baselines. “Self-RAG trigger signal” denotes the trigger evaluated on the same reader table, not a full Self-RAG pipeline.
Table 3: Held-out threshold deployment for near-match operating points. Thresholds are selected on validation folds and evaluated once on test folds.
Figure 2: Scaling trend on TriviaQA. Larger readers can skip more retrieval while maintaining always-RAG accuracy.
Figure 5: Cost-regime analysis. Curves substitute model-specific measured cCB and vary ρ = cret/cOB in Equation 7; gating is faster only above the resulting break-even skip threshold.
Table 7: Calibration method comparison for sequence log-probability.
Figure 8: Three-action view of skip, retrieve, and abstain. Accuracy is computed over answered queries, so it should be read together with coverage.
Figure 4: Selective RAG risk-coverage curves. Proper chosen-answer confidence helps on TriviaQA and NQ; MS MARCO is a low-headroom boundary case.
Figure 3: Cross-family and cross-dataset behavior. The gate is strongest when the reader has meaningful closed-book headroom but retrieval still provides rescue opportunities.
Figure 7: Motivation from PopQA: parametric memory and retrieval value vary with popularity.
Table 1: Calibration quality for Qwen3-8B. Raw metrics use min-max normalized scores; calibrated metrics use diagnostic out-of-fold logistic probabilities. Lower is better for ECE/Brier/NLL.
